title: Template for Line Graph (Task 1)
structure: Introduction: Paraphrase the task.
Overview: Highlight main trends.
Body Paragraph 1: Describe first trend or group.
Body Paragraph 2: Describe second trend or group.
Conclusion: Brief summary (if appropriate for your exam format).

phrases.introduction: The line graph illustrates changes in...

example: The line graph illustrates changes in energy consumption from 1990 to 2020. Overall, it is evident that renewable sources grew significantly.

title: Table Task — comparison pattern
structure: Introduction: State what the table shows.
Overview: Identify the largest differences or rankings.
Body 1: Compare key figures for category A.
Body 2: Compare key figures for category B.

phrases.overview: The most striking feature is that...
phrases.body: Whereas X stood at..., Y recorded...
phrases.conclusion: In summary, the figures reveal clear disparities.

example: The table provides data regarding household ownership of consumer goods. The most striking feature is that internet access rose most sharply.

title: Process diagram — stages
structure: Introduction: Paraphrase the diagram purpose.
Overview: Summarise the main stages.
Body: Describe each stage in sequence using passive voice.

phrases.introduction: The diagram illustrates the process by which...
phrases.overview: Overall, the process consists of several distinct stages.

example: The diagram illustrates the process by which rainwater is collected. Initially, rainwater runs off the roof into a drain.

title: Formal complaint letter (GT-style)
structure: Opening: Purpose of writing.
Paragraph 1: Describe the problem clearly.
Paragraph 2: Explain impact and previous steps taken.
Closing: State desired outcome and polite close.

phrases.introduction: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with...
phrases.overview: During my stay on [date], I encountered several issues.
phrases.body: Specifically, ... / Despite having mentioned this to...
phrases.conclusion: I would appreciate a partial refund and assurance that...

example: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the service I received at your hotel on 12 March.

title: Opinion essay — agree/disagree
structure: Introduction: Topic + clear thesis (extent of agreement).
Body 1: Strongest argument supporting your view.
Body 2: Second argument or acknowledge counter-argument briefly.
Conclusion: Restate position clearly.

phrases.introduction: It is argued that... This essay strongly agrees that...
phrases.body: Firstly, ... / Furthermore, ... / Admittedly, some may argue...
phrases.conclusion: In conclusion, I firmly believe that...

example: It is argued that unpaid community service should be compulsory. This essay strongly agrees that such programmes develop civic responsibility.

title: Discussion essay — both views + opinion
structure: Introduction: Paraphrase + outline both sides.
Body 1: View A with explanation.
Body 2: View B with explanation.
Conclusion: Balanced summary + your opinion.

phrases.introduction: While some people believe..., others contend that...
phrases.body: On the one hand... / On the other hand...
phrases.conclusion: In my view, although..., I believe that...

example: While some people believe dangerous sports should be banned, others contend that adults should decide for themselves.

title: Problem and solution
structure: Introduction: State the issue in general terms.
Body 1: Causes or why the problem exists.
Body 2: Practical solutions with examples.
Conclusion: Summarise problem + best responses.

phrases.introduction: Traffic congestion has become a pressing issue in many urban areas.
phrases.body: One major cause is... / Effective measures could include...
phrases.conclusion: To tackle this, governments and individuals must work together.

example: Traffic congestion has become a pressing issue. One major cause is reliance on private cars; effective measures could include expanding public transport.

title: Bar chart — groups and trends
structure: Introduction: What is compared.
Overview: Highest/lowest categories or overall pattern.
Body: Detail comparisons with data.

phrases.overview: Overall, X accounted for the largest share throughout the period.
phrases.body: City A experienced a rise from... to..., whereas...

example: The bar chart compares average monthly temperatures in two cities. Overall, City B was consistently warmer in mid-year months.

title: Map comparison
structure: Introduction: Area and time period shown.
Overview: Main developments (e.g. expansion, new facilities).
Body: Describe north/south or key zones of change.

phrases.introduction: The maps show how [place] developed between...
phrases.overview: The village underwent significant expansion, particularly to the east.
phrases.body: A new road was constructed... / The former farmland was replaced by...

example: The maps show how a coastal village developed between 1980 and 2020. The village underwent significant expansion east of the original centre.

title: Strong opinion — partial agreement
structure: Introduction: Nuanced thesis (partially agree).
Body 1: Aspect you support.
Body 2: Aspect you qualify or reject.
Conclusion: Balanced but decisive closing.

phrases.introduction: I partially agree with the notion that...
phrases.body: This is because... / Nevertheless, it should be noted that...
phrases.conclusion: Therefore, while..., a more balanced approach would...

example: I partially agree that longer sentences reduce crime, because deterrence matters; nevertheless, rehabilitation cannot be ignored.

title: Pie charts — proportions
structure: Introduction: Variables and years.
Overview: Largest segments and main shifts.
Body: Compare proportions with approximate figures.

phrases.introduction: The pie charts depict the proportion of...
phrases.overview: The share of renewables increased noticeably over the period.
phrases.body: In 2000, fossil fuels represented roughly...%, but by 2020 this had fallen to...

example: The pie charts depict the proportion of energy from different sources in 2000 and 2020. The share of renewables increased noticeably.

title: Direct question — causes + opinion on support
structure: Introduction: Restate both questions from the prompt.
Body 1: Reasons (e.g. globalisation, marketing).
Body 2: Whether local film should be subsidised + justification.
Conclusion: Short synthesis.

phrases.introduction: The popularity of foreign films raises questions about cultural preferences and policy.
phrases.body: One reason for this trend is... / Governments arguably should... because...
phrases.conclusion: Thus, both consumer choice and cultural identity need consideration.

example: One reason for preferring foreign films is wider distribution budgets; governments arguably should support local cinema to preserve cultural narratives.
